posts.title: Building a Real-time Vehicle Counting System with YOLO11
posts.excerpt: A comprehensive guide to creating a production-ready vehicle detection and counting system using YOLO11, BotSORT tracking, and Streamlit. Learn how to detect multiple vehicle types, count them in both directions, and deploy as a web app...
posts.author: Mahadi Hasan Shaisob
posts.content:
Introduction

Vehicle detection and counting is a crucial task in traffic monitoring, urban planning, and smart city applications. Traditional methods using inductive loops or infrared sensors are expensive to install and maintain. Computer vision offers a cost-effective alternative that can be deployed using existing CCTV infrastructure.

In this comprehensive guide, I'll walk you through how I built a real-time vehicle counting system using YOLO11 and Streamlit. This system can detect multiple vehicle types, count them in both directions, and provide real-time analytics through a beautiful web interface.

The Problem Statement

Traditional vehicle counting methods face several challenges: high installation costs, limited vehicle types, single direction only, and maintenance issues.

Results

After extensive testing on diverse traffic videos, the system achieved 95.8% overall accuracy with 97.2% for car detection.

posts.title: XSS Payload Detection Website: Building a Real-time Deep Learning Detection System
posts.excerpt: A comprehensive guide to building a production-ready XSS detection system using Deep Learning (CNN-BiLSTM). Learn how I trained models on a large-scale dataset of 100,000+ obfuscated XSS payloads and deployed a real-time detection website that predicts malicious payloads in milliseconds...
posts.author: Mahadi Hasan Shaisob
posts.content:
The Motivation Behind This Project

Cross-site scripting (XSS) remains one of the most prevalent web security vulnerabilities, affecting nearly 80% of websites according to OWASP. Traditional detection methods like signature-based and rule-based approaches struggle to identify obfuscated attack payloads that attackers use to evade detection. This motivated me to build a robust, real-time XSS detection system using Deep Learning.

I developed a production-ready web application that can classify any input payload as either Malicious (XSS) or Benign in milliseconds, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy of 98.1% using a BiLSTM architecture.

🎯 Live Demo: Try it yourself at https://xssdetectionwithobfuscated.streamlit.app

The Research Dataset

One of the most critical aspects of this project was creating a comprehensive, high-quality dataset. I constructed a large-scale annotated XSS dataset containing:

  • 100,000+ XSS payloads covering all major attack types
  • Obfuscated Payloads - Generated using CodeT5 LLM to simulate real-world evasive techniques
  • Reflected XSS - Payloads that reflect off web servers
  • DOM-based XSS - Client-side vulnerabilities
  • Stored XSS - Persistent attacks stored in databases
  • Benign samples - Normal user inputs for balanced training
📊 Dataset Access: The complete dataset is publicly available on IEEE Dataport: Large-scale Annotated Dataset for XSS Attack Detection

How Deep Learning Solves XSS Detection

Traditional methods rely on predefined patterns and signatures. Attackers easily bypass these by obfuscating their payloads. Deep Learning models learn the underlying patterns of malicious code rather than specific strings.

The Challenge: Training Time vs. Inference Speed

A common question arises: 'Deep learning models take hours to train, but how can they detect XSS in milliseconds?'

The answer lies in the separation of training and inference phases:

  1. Training Phase (Offline, once):
    • The model processes thousands/millions of examples
    • It learns patterns, weights, and biases through backpropagation
    • This takes hours or even days on GPUs
    • The result is a trained model file containing optimized weights
  2. Inference Phase (Online, every prediction):
    • The saved model weights are loaded into memory
    • Each new input passes through the already-trained neural network
    • Only forward propagation happens (no backpropagation)
    • This takes milliseconds!
⚡ Key Insight: The .sav file (pickle/serialized model) contains the learned weights and architecture. Loading this file is like loading a pre-trained brain - it already knows how to detect XSS without re-learning!

The Complete Workflow

1. Training Pipeline (Offline - Run Once)

import pandas as pd
import pickle
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer
from tensorflow.keras.models import Sequential
from tensorflow.keras.layers import Embedding, Conv1D, LSTM, Dense

# Step 1: Load dataset of 100,000+ XSS payloads
df = pd.read_csv('xss_dataset.csv')
X = df['payload']
y = df['label']

# Step 2: Character-level TF-IDF vectorization
tfidf_vectorizer_char = TfidfVectorizer(
    analyzer='char',
    ngram_range=(2, 5),  # Character n-grams
    max_features=5000
)
X_features = tfidf_vectorizer_char.fit_transform(X)

# Step 3: Build CNN-BiLSTM architecture
model = Sequential([
    Embedding(input_dim=5000, output_dim=128, input_length=500),
    Conv1D(64, 3, activation='relu'),
    Bidirectional(LSTM(64, return_sequences=True)),
    Bidirectional(LSTM(32)),
    Dense(64, activation='relu'),
    Dropout(0.5),
    Dense(1, activation='sigmoid')
])

# Step 4: Train the model (THIS TAKES HOURS!)
model.compile(optimizer='adam', loss='binary_crossentropy', metrics=['accuracy'])
model.fit(X_features, y, epochs=20, batch_size=32, validation_split=0.2)

# Step 5: Save the trained model and vectorizer
pickle.dump(model, open('trained_xss_char.sav', 'wb'))
pickle.dump(tfidf_vectorizer_char, open('tfidf_vectorizer_char.sav', 'wb'))

print('✅ Model saved! Training complete.')

2. Inference Code (Real-time - Milliseconds)

# xss_prediction.py - The lightweight prediction script
import pickle

# Load pre-trained model and vectorizer (once at startup)
loaded_model = pickle.load(open('trained_xss_char.sav', 'rb'))
tfidf_vectorizer_char = pickle.load(open('tfidf_vectorizer_char.sav', 'rb'))

def xss_prediction(payload):
    '''
    Predict XSS (1) or benign (0) in milliseconds!
    No training involved - just forward pass through saved weights.
    '''
    # Transform input using saved vectorizer
    features = tfidf_vectorizer_char.transform([payload])
    
    # Forward pass through pre-trained model
    prediction = loaded_model.predict(features)
    
    if prediction[0] == 1:
        return '🔴 Malicious - XSS Payload Detected!'
    else:
        return '🟢 Benign - No XSS Detected'

# Example - Detects in milliseconds!
test_payload = ''
result = xss_prediction(test_payload)
print(result)  # 🔴 Malicious - XSS Payload Detected!
💡 Why So Fast? The .sav file contains the model's learned weights. Loading it is like loading a program - it's ready to run immediately. Each prediction is just a simple mathematical calculation through the network!

Character-Level vs Word-Level Processing

A key innovation in this project is using character-level n-grams instead of word-level tokenization. Here's why this matters:

  • Word-level: Tokenizes on spaces (e.g., 'alert', 'script') - easy to obfuscate!
  • Character-level: Breaks down to characters (e.g., 'a','l','e','r','t') - captures obfuscation patterns!
# Word-level (easily bypassed)
'alert' vs 'al\x65rt' -> Different words, same meaning!

# Character-level (captures pattern)
'alert' -> ['a','l','e','r','t'] 
'al\x65rt' -> ['a','l','\x65','r','t']
# Pattern similarity is detected!

Deployment with Streamlit

I created a production-ready web application using Streamlit. Here's the complete app structure:

# xss_app.py - Full Streamlit Application
import pickle
import streamlit as st

# Load model (once when app starts)
loaded_model = pickle.load(open('trained_xss_char.sav', 'rb'))
tfidf_vectorizer_char = pickle.load(open('tfidf_vectorizer_char.sav', 'rb'))

def xss_prediction(payload):
    features = tfidf_vectorizer_char.transform([payload])
    prediction = loaded_model.predict(features)
    return prediction[0]

def main():
    st.set_page_config(page_title='XSS Detection App', page_icon='🛡️')
    st.title('🛡️ XSS Payload Detection Web App')
    
    payload = st.text_area('Enter payload to check:', height=100)
    
    if st.button('🔍 Detect XSS'):
        result = xss_prediction(payload)
        if result == 1:
            st.error('⚠️ MALICIOUS: XSS Payload Detected!')
        else:
            st.success('✅ SAFE: No XSS Detected')

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()

Results and Performance

Model Accuracy on Test Dataset:

ModelAccuracyPrecisionRecallF1-Score
BiLSTM (Proposed)98.1%97.8%98.3%98.0%
CNN97.22%96.9%97.5%97.2%
XGBoost (Baseline)89.3%88.7%89.9%89.3%

Publications and Research Impact

This research has been published and presented at prestigious international conferences:

📄 Paper Information

Title: DeepGuard-XSS: Leveraging Large Language Models with CNN–BiLSTM for Robust Detection of Obfuscated XSS Attacks

Conference: 2026 5th International Conference on Electrical, Computer & Telecommunication Engineering (ICECTE), Rajshahi, Bangladesh (January 29-31, 2026)

IEEE Xplore: 10.1109/ICECTE69292.2026.11429351

Dataset: IEEE Dataport - XSS Detection Dataset

How You Can Use This System

1. Try the Live Demo:

Visit https://xssdetectionwithobfuscated.streamlit.app and test any payload!

2. Test Examples:

  • Malicious: <script>alert('XSS')</script> → 🔴 XSS Detected
  • Malicious (Obfuscated): <ScRiPt>alert(String.fromCharCode(88,83,83))</ScRiPt> → 🔴 XSS Detected
  • Benign: Hello, this is a normal comment → 🟢 Benign

3. Integrate into Your Application:

# Python integration example
from xss_prediction import xss_prediction

user_input = request.form['comment']
result = xss_prediction(user_input)

if 'Malicious' in result:
    return 'Blocked: XSS detected!'
else:
    save_to_database(user_input)

Conclusion

This project demonstrates that Deep Learning can effectively detect even the most sophisticated obfuscated XSS attacks in real-time. 🔗 Resources:

  • Live Demo: https://xssdetectionwithobfuscated.streamlit.app
  • GitHub Repository: github.com/shoisob2004037/streamlit_xss_detection_with_obfuscated
  • IEEE Paper: Read on IEEE Xplore
  • Dataset: Download from IEEE Dataport

posts.title: Complete MERN Stack Project Setup: A Beginner's Guide to Building Scalable Web Apps
posts.excerpt: A complete guide to setting up a MERN (MongoDB, Express.js, React, Node.js) stack project from scratch. Learn how to structure your project, handle media uploads with Cloudinary, implement MVC pattern, secure your app with environment variables, and build production-ready applications...
posts.author: Mahadi Hasan Shaisob
posts.content:
What is MERN Stack?

MERN stack is a powerful JavaScript stack used for building full-stack web applications. It consists of four main technologies:

  • MongoDB - NoSQL database for storing application data
  • Express.js - Backend web framework for Node.js
  • React.js - Frontend library for building user interfaces
  • Node.js - JavaScript runtime for server-side programming

In this comprehensive guide, I'll walk you through setting up a complete MERN stack project with best practices, including Cloudinary for media storage, MVC architecture, and environment variables for security.

Project Structure

Here's the recommended folder structure for a MERN stack project:

mern-project/
├── client/                 # Frontend React application
│   ├── public/
│   ├── src/
│   │   ├── components/
│   │   ├── pages/
│   │   ├── services/
│   │   ├── utils/
│   │   ├── App.jsx
│   │   └── main.jsx
│   ├── .env
│   ├── package.json
│   └── vite.config.js
├── server/                 # Backend Node/Express application
│   ├── models/
│   ├── controllers/
│   ├── routes/
│   ├── middleware/
│   ├── config/
│   ├── uploads/
│   ├── .env
│   ├── server.js
│   └── package.json
└── README.md

Step 1: Setting Up Environment Variables (.env)

Environment variables are crucial for security. Never commit your .env files to GitHub!

Client .env file (client/.env):

VITE_API_URL=http://localhost:5000/api
VITE_CLOUDINARY_CLOUD_NAME=your_cloud_name
VITE_CLOUDINARY_UPLOAD_PRESET=your_upload_preset

Server .env file (server/.env):

PORT=5000
MONGODB_URI=mongodb+srv://username:password@cluster.mongodb.net/database
JWT_SECRET=your_super_secret_jwt_key_here
JWT_EXPIRE=30d
CLOUDINARY_CLOUD_NAME=your_cloud_name
CLOUDINARY_API_KEY=your_api_key
CLOUDINARY_API_SECRET=your_api_secret
CLIENT_URL=http://localhost:5173
NODE_ENV=development
⚠️ Important: Always add .env files to .gitignore to prevent exposing sensitive information!

Step 2: Setting Up the Backend (Server)

Initialize the server:

cd server
npm init -y
npm install express mongoose cors dotenv bcryptjs jsonwebtoken cloudinary multer
npm install -D nodemon

Update package.json scripts:

"scripts": {
  "start": "node server.js",
  "dev": "nodemon server.js"
}

Basic server.js setup:

// server/server.js
const express = require('express');
const mongoose = require('mongoose');
const cors = require('cors');
const dotenv = require('dotenv');
const cloudinary = require('cloudinary').v2;

dotenv.config();

const app = express();

// CORS configuration
app.use(cors({
  origin: process.env.CLIENT_URL || 'http://localhost:5173',
  credentials: true
}));

// Middleware
app.use(express.json());
app.use(express.urlencoded({ extended: true }));

// Cloudinary configuration
cloudinary.config({
  cloud_name: process.env.CLOUDINARY_CLOUD_NAME,
  api_key: process.env.CLOUDINARY_API_KEY,
  api_secret: process.env.CLOUDINARY_API_SECRET
});

// MongoDB connection
mongoose.connect(process.env.MONGODB_URI)
  .then(() => console.log('MongoDB connected'))
  .catch(err => console.log('MongoDB connection error:', err));

// Routes
app.use('/api/auth', require('./routes/authRoutes'));
app.use('/api/posts', require('./routes/postRoutes'));
app.use('/api/upload', require('./routes/uploadRoutes'));

// Health check
app.get('/api/health', (req, res) => {
  res.json({ message: 'Server is running', status: 'ok' });
});

// Error handling middleware
app.use((err, req, res, next) => {
  console.error(err.stack);
  res.status(err.status || 500).json({
    message: err.message || 'Something went wrong!',
    error: process.env.NODE_ENV === 'development' ? err : {}
  });
});

const PORT = process.env.PORT || 5000;
app.listen(PORT, () => {
  console.log(`Server running on port ${PORT}`);
});

Step 3: Understanding MVC Pattern

The MVC (Model-View-Controller) pattern helps organize code into three interconnected components:

Model (models/Post.js):

// server/models/Post.js
const mongoose = require('mongoose');

const postSchema = new mongoose.Schema({
  title: {
    type: String,
    required: [true, 'Title is required'],
    trim: true
  },
  content: {
    type: String,
    required: [true, 'Content is required']
  },
  imageUrl: {
    type: String,
    default: ''
  },
  cloudinaryId: {
    type: String,
    default: ''
  },
  author: {
    type: mongoose.Schema.Types.ObjectId,
    ref: 'User',
    required: true
  }
}, {
  timestamps: true
});

module.exports = mongoose.model('Post', postSchema);

Controller (controllers/postController.js):

// server/controllers/postController.js
const Post = require('../models/Post');
const cloudinary = require('cloudinary').v2;

// Create a new post
const createPost = async (req, res) => {
  try {
    const { title, content, imageUrl, cloudinaryId } = req.body;
    const post = await Post.create({
      title,
      content,
      imageUrl,
      cloudinaryId,
      author: req.user.id
    });
    res.status(201).json({ success: true, data: post });
  } catch (error) {
    res.status(500).json({ success: false, message: error.message });
  }
};

// Get all posts
const getAllPosts = async (req, res) => {
  try {
    const posts = await Post.find().populate('author', 'name email').sort({ createdAt: -1 });
    res.status(200).json({ success: true, data: posts });
  } catch (error) {
    res.status(500).json({ success: false, message: error.message });
  }
};

// Get single post
const getPostById = async (req, res) => {
  try {
    const post = await Post.findById(req.params.id).populate('author', 'name email');
    if (!post) {
      return res.status(404).json({ success: false, message: 'Post not found' });
    }
    res.status(200).json({ success: true, data: post });
  } catch (error) {
    res.status(500).json({ success: false, message: error.message });
  }
};

// Update post
const updatePost = async (req, res) => {
  try {
    const post = await Post.findById(req.params.id);
    if (!post) {
      return res.status(404).json({ success: false, message: 'Post not found' });
    }
    
    // If new image is uploaded, delete old from Cloudinary
    if (req.body.cloudinaryId && post.cloudinaryId) {
      await cloudinary.uploader.destroy(post.cloudinaryId);
    }
    
    const updatedPost = await Post.findByIdAndUpdate(
      req.params.id,
      req.body,
      { new: true, runValidators: true }
    );
    
    res.status(200).json({ success: true, data: updatedPost });
  } catch (error) {
    res.status(500).json({ success: false, message: error.message });
  }
};

// Delete post (remove from Cloudinary too)
const deletePost = async (req, res) => {
  try {
    const post = await Post.findById(req.params.id);
    if (!post) {
      return res.status(404).json({ success: false, message: 'Post not found' });
    }
    
    // Delete from Cloudinary
    if (post.cloudinaryId) {
      await cloudinary.uploader.destroy(post.cloudinaryId);
    }
    
    await post.deleteOne();
    res.status(200).json({ success: true, message: 'Post deleted successfully' });
  } catch (error) {
    res.status(500).json({ success: false, message: error.message });
  }
};

module.exports = {
  createPost,
  getAllPosts,
  getPostById,
  updatePost,
  deletePost
};

Routes (routes/postRoutes.js):

// server/routes/postRoutes.js
const express = require('express');
const router = express.Router();
const {
  createPost,
  getAllPosts,
  getPostById,
  updatePost,
  deletePost
} = require('../controllers/postController');
const { protect } = require('../middleware/authMiddleware');

router.route('/')
  .get(getAllPosts)
  .post(protect, createPost);

router.route('/:id')
  .get(getPostById)
  .put(protect, updatePost)
  .delete(protect, deletePost);

module.exports = router;

Step 4: Cloudinary Setup for Media Storage

Cloudinary is used for storing images, videos, and other media files. Here's how to set it up:

Cloudinary upload middleware (middleware/uploadMiddleware.js):

// server/middleware/uploadMiddleware.js
const multer = require('multer');
const { CloudinaryStorage } = require('multer-storage-cloudinary');
const cloudinary = require('cloudinary').v2;

// Configure Cloudinary storage
const storage = new CloudinaryStorage({
  cloudinary: cloudinary,
  params: {
    folder: 'mern_posts',
    allowed_formats: ['jpg', 'png', 'jpeg', 'gif', 'webp', 'mp4'],
    transformation: [{ width: 1200, height: 800, crop: 'limit' }]
  }
});

const upload = multer({
  storage: storage,
  limits: { fileSize: 10 * 1024 * 1024 }, // 10MB limit
  fileFilter: (req, file, cb) => {
    if (file.mimetype.startsWith('image/') || file.mimetype.startsWith('video/')) {
      cb(null, true);
    } else {
      cb(new Error('Only images and videos are allowed!'), false);
    }
  }
});

module.exports = upload;

Upload route (routes/uploadRoutes.js):

// server/routes/uploadRoutes.js
const express = require('express');
const router = express.Router();
const upload = require('../middleware/uploadMiddleware');
const { protect } = require('../middleware/authMiddleware');

// Single file upload
router.post('/single', protect, upload.single('file'), (req, res) => {
  try {
    if (!req.file) {
      return res.status(400).json({ message: 'No file uploaded' });
    }
    res.json({
      url: req.file.path,
      cloudinaryId: req.file.filename,
      message: 'File uploaded successfully'
    });
  } catch (error) {
    res.status(500).json({ message: error.message });
  }
});

// Multiple files upload
router.post('/multiple', protect, upload.array('files', 5), (req, res) => {
  try {
    if (!req.files || req.files.length === 0) {
      return res.status(400).json({ message: 'No files uploaded' });
    }
    const files = req.files.map(file => ({
      url: file.path,
      cloudinaryId: file.filename
    }));
    res.json({ files, message: 'Files uploaded successfully' });
  } catch (error) {
    res.status(500).json({ message: error.message });
  }
});

module.exports = router;

Step 5: Setting Up the Frontend (Client)

Initialize the client with Vite:

cd client
npm create vite@latest . -- --template react
npm install axios react-router-dom tailwindcss lucide-react
npm install -D tailwindcss postcss autoprefixer
npx tailwindcss init -p

Configure Tailwind CSS (tailwind.config.js):

/** @type {import('tailwindcss').Config} */
export default {
  content: [
    "./index.html",
    "./src/**/*.{js,ts,jsx,tsx}",
  ],
  darkMode: 'class',
  theme: {
    extend: {},
  },
  plugins: [],
}

Add to index.css:

@tailwind base;
@tailwind components;
@tailwind utilities;

API service (src/services/api.js):

// src/services/api.js
import axios from 'axios';

const API_URL = import.meta.env.VITE_API_URL || 'http://localhost:5000/api';

const api = axios.create({
  baseURL: API_URL,
  headers: {
    'Content-Type': 'application/json',
  },
});

// Add token to requests
api.interceptors.request.use(
  (config) => {
    const token = localStorage.getItem('token');
    if (token) {
      config.headers.Authorization = `Bearer ${token}`;
    }
    return config;
  },
  (error) => Promise.reject(error)
);

export default api;

Cloudinary upload helper (src/utils/upload.js):

// src/utils/upload.js
const CLOUDINARY_CLOUD_NAME = import.meta.env.VITE_CLOUDINARY_CLOUD_NAME;
const CLOUDINARY_UPLOAD_PRESET = import.meta.env.VITE_CLOUDINARY_UPLOAD_PRESET;

export const uploadToCloudinary = async (file) => {
  const formData = new FormData();
  formData.append('file', file);
  formData.append('upload_preset', CLOUDINARY_UPLOAD_PRESET);

  try {
    const response = await fetch(
      `https://api.cloudinary.com/v1_1/${CLOUDINARY_CLOUD_NAME}/image/upload`,
      { method: 'POST', body: formData }
    );
    const data = await response.json();
    return { url: data.secure_url, cloudinaryId: data.public_id };
  } catch (error) {
    console.error('Upload error:', error);
    throw error;
  }
};

Step 6: Complete Workflow

  1. User uploads a file → File sent to Cloudinary
  2. Cloudinary returns URL and public_id → Stored in MongoDB with the post
  3. When fetching posts → MongoDB sends the Cloudinary URL
  4. Frontend displays images → Using the Cloudinary URL from the database
Pro Tip: Always store both the Cloudinary URL AND the public_id in your database. The URL is for display, the public_id is needed for deleting or updating the file later!

Benefits of This Architecture

  • Scalability: Cloudinary handles media storage, reducing server load
  • Security: Environment variables keep sensitive data safe
  • Maintainability: MVC pattern makes code organized and easy to update
  • Reusability: Models, controllers, and services can be reused across projects
  • Performance: Cloudinary serves optimized images with automatic compression

Conclusion

This complete MERN stack setup provides a solid foundation for building production-ready web applications. The combination of MongoDB for data storage, Express/Node for the backend, React for the frontend, and Cloudinary for media management creates a powerful, scalable, and maintainable full-stack solution.

Remember to always keep your .env files secure, follow the MVC pattern for clean code organization, and leverage Cloudinary for all your media storage needs. Happy coding! 🚀
